one_line_takeaway: SKILLJECT automates skill-based prompt injection via a two-channel design (hidden payload in auxiliary scripts + front-loaded, feedback-refined inducement text in SKILL.md) and drives Attack Success Rate on real skill-enabled coding agents from 0% (naive direct injection) to 68-97% depending on backend, showing that reusable agent-skill ecosystems are a scalable supply-chain attack surface distinct from ordinary indirect prompt injection.
key_finding: On Claude Code (Table I), the Naive baseline achieves 0.0% ASR across all four backends and all four attack categories, while SKILLJECT achieves an average overall ASR of 80.7%, with per-category averages of 78.8% (InfoDisc), 81.0% (PrivEsc), 83.5% (UnauWri), 79.5% (Backdoor); per-backend overall ASRs are 97.2% (GLM-4.7), 94.7% (MiniMax-M2.1), 83.8% (GPT-5-mini), and 47.0% (Claude-Sonnet-4.6, the most resistant). On OpenClaw (Table II) the pattern replicates: Naive 0% across the board, SKILLJECT overall 80.5% (97.0% GLM-4.7, 95.0% MiniMax-M2.1, 87.0% GPT-5-mini, 43.0% Claude-Sonnet-4.6). On newer frontier backends (Table III, 30-skill subset) SKILLJECT averages 68.4% overall, remaining high on DeepSeek-V4-flash (95.8%), GLM-5.1 (92.4%), MiniMax-M2.7 (90.2%), MiMo-V2.7 (74.3%), but dropping sharply on GPT-5.4 (28.7%) and Claude-Opus-4.6 (29.2%), while Naive stays at 0.0% throughout. Head-to-head against Skill-Inject (Table V, same benign skills and same malicious scripts) SKILLJECT raises overall ASR from 32.3% to 69.1%, with per-backend gains of 40.0%->82.2% (GLM-4.7), 35.6%->80.9% (MiniMax-M2.1), 26.7%->64.4% (GPT-5-mini), 26.7%->48.9% (Claude-Sonnet-4.6). Cross-model transfer of skills generated on GLM-4.7 (Table VI) retains 79.8% overall ASR on MiniMax-M2.1, 49.8% on GPT-5-mini, and 35.8% on Claude-Sonnet-4.6 -- drops of 17.4, 47.4, and 61.4 percentage points respectively relative to the 97.2% source-model result, showing partial but imperfect transfer. Cross-task transfer on GLM-4.7 (Fig. 4) drops from 98%/98%/95%/98% (Backdoor/InfoDisc/PrivEsc/UnauWri under the original task instruction) to 80%/72%/77%/70% on 20 additional unseen task instructions for the same skill. The refinement ablation (Fig. 6) shows ASR climbing over 5 iterations to 97.2% (GLM-4.7), 94.6% (MiniMax-M2.1), 83.8% (GPT-5-mini), and 52.8% (Claude-Sonnet-4.6), with most gains front-loaded in early iterations. The SNS analysis (Fig. 7, GLM-4.7) shows SKILLJECT scoring higher naturalness than Naive in every category (e.g., 0.429 vs 0.223 for Backdoor, 0.415 vs 0.318 for InfoDisc, 0.429 vs 0.340 for PrivEsc, 0.462 vs 0.259 for UnauWri), i.e., the injected documentation is judged to blend more naturally into the original skill than a naive rewrite.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SKILLJECT, the first automated framework for generating and iteratively refining poisoned agent 'skills' (Anthropic-style SKILL.md packages plus auxiliary scripts) so that skill-enabled coding agents (Claude Code, OpenClaw) actually execute a hidden malicious payload. SKILLJECT splits the attack into two coordinated channels: an artifact channel that hides the payload inside an auxiliary helper script (.py/.sh), and an instruction channel that rewrites SKILL.md with a 'front-loaded inducement' strategy -- placing the injected instruction at the very start of the document, framing the helper script as a mandatory prerequisite/required first step, and giving an explicit path and example command. Generation and refinement are run by a closed-loop multi-agent pipeline of three LLM roles: an Attack Agent (generates/edits the poisoned SKILL.md, conditioned on a 'payload understanding' module that analyzes the helper script), a Victim Agent (executes downstream tasks with the poisoned skill inside a sandboxed agent platform), and an Evaluate Agent (inspects execution traces to output a binary success signal plus a diagnostic label -- ignored, refused, partially triggered, or incorrectly executed -- that is fed back to the Attack Agent for the next refinement iteration, while the hidden payload script itself is kept fixed across iterations).
threat_model: Black-box attacker who can publish/distribute a poisoned skill package through channels the victim agent can reach (public skill registries such as ClawHub, shared repos, importable bundles) and can modify both SKILL.md and bundled artifacts before distribution, but cannot intervene in the victim agent's execution after installation, cannot control the victim's router/model parameters/system prompt/safety mechanisms, and cannot force the router to select the poisoned skill -- the attack only has effect when the poisoned skill is selected and run under a relevant task. Attack success is defined at the trace level: a predicate M(tau;B) returns 1 if the victim agent's execution trace shows explicit execution of the hidden helper script associated with a target behavior category B, independent of whether the full downstream harmful consequence (e.g., actual exfiltration) is realized -- the paper is explicit that real-world impact would additionally depend on runtime permissions, sandboxing, network access, and user approval policies not modeled here. Four target behavior categories are evaluated: Information Disclosure (exfiltrating env vars like AWS_ACCESS_KEY or local files to an attacker server), Privilege Escalation (altering system configs/file permissions), Unauthorized Write (modifying/deleting/encrypting files outside the authorized workspace), and Backdoor Injection (executing a script or presenting a malicious URL to induce user interaction).
limitations: The paper's own explicit caveats: ASR is measured strictly at the payload-execution trace level (did the hidden helper script run), not at the level of realized downstream harm, which the authors note additionally depends on deployment-specific permissions, sandboxing, network access, and runtime enforcement not modeled in the benchmark. Frontier/safety-aligned models (GPT-5.4, Claude-Opus-4.6) show markedly lower ASR (~29%), which the authors attribute to stronger tool-use caution and safety alignment, indicating the attack is not uniformly effective against best-in-class models. Cross-model and cross-task transfer both degrade substantially relative to same-model/same-task performance, showing the generated attacks are not fully general and benefit from target-specific refinement. The frontier-model comparison (Table III) is run on only a 30-skill random subset rather than the full 100-skill benchmark, explicitly due to evaluation cost. The Attack Agent used throughout is GPT-3.5-Turbo, a comparatively weak/older generator model, which the authors do not discuss as a potential limitation on attack ceiling. The comparison baseline Skill-Inject is evaluated only under one of its settings ('Obvious Injections with Body + Script') rather than its full evaluation suite.

datasets_used: 100 real agent skills scraped from ClawHub (public skill-sharing platform) across four domains: Software Development & DevOps, Data & Financial Analysis, Content Creation & Marketing, Research & Agent Tooling
30-skill random subset of the above for the frontier-model comparison (Table III)
quantitative_results: Claude Code, average overall ASR: Naive 0.0% vs SKILLJECT 80.7% (Table I)
Claude Code per-category ASR (SKILLJECT): InfoDisc 78.8%, PrivEsc 81.0%, UnauWri 83.5%, Backdoor 79.5%
Claude Code per-backend overall ASR (SKILLJECT): GLM-4.7 97.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 94.7%, GPT-5-mini 83.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 47.0%
OpenClaw overall ASR: Naive 0% vs SKILLJECT 80.5% (Table II); per-backend GLM-4.7 97.0%, MiniMax-M2.1 95.0%, GPT-5-mini 87.0%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 43.0%

Frontier backends (Table III), 30-skill subset: SKILLJECT overall 68.4% vs Naive 0.0%; DeepSeek-V4-flash 95.8%, GLM-5.1 92.4%, MiniMax-M2.7 90.2%, MiMo-V2.7 74.3%, GPT-5.4 28.7%, Claude-Opus-4.6 29.2%
Paradigm comparison (Table IV) overall ASR: Prior Manual Attack 32.3%, Naive injection 0.0%, SKILLJECT 69.1%
Head-to-head vs Skill-Inject (Table V) overall ASR: 32.3% -> 69.1%; per-backend GLM-4.7 40.0%->82.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 35.6%->80.9%, GPT-5-mini 26.7%->64.4%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 26.7%->48.9%
Cross-model transfer from GLM-4.7 source (Table VI) overall ASR: MiniMax-M2.1 79.8%, GPT-5-mini 49.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 35.8% (drops of 17.4/47.4/61.4 pp vs 97.2% source performance)
Cross-task transferability on GLM-4.7 (Fig. 4): original-task ASR 98%/98%/95%/98% (Backdoor/InfoDisc/PrivEsc/UnauWri) vs cross-task ASR 80%/72%/77%/70%
Refinement-iteration ablation (Fig. 6), ASR after 5 iterations: GLM-4.7 97.2%, MiniMax-M2.1 94.6%, GPT-5-mini 83.8%, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 52.8%
Structural Naturalness Score, GLM-4.7 (Fig. 7): Naive vs SKILLJECT -- Backdoor 0.223 vs 0.429, InfoDisc 0.318 vs 0.415, PrivEsc 0.340 vs 0.429, UnauWri 0.259 vs 0.462

Preliminary motivating study (Fig. 2): response distribution of malicious instructions in publicly collected (unrefined) skills across 4 agents shows Attack Success rates of only 2.5-7.5%, with the rest Rejected or Ignored -- motivating the need for SKILLJECT's automated refinement
baselines_compared: Naive / Direct Injection: malicious commands written directly into SKILL.md with no payload hiding, obfuscation, or helper scripts
Skill-Inject [31]: prior manually constructed malicious-skill-file attack, compared under its 'Obvious Injections with Body + Script' setting using identical clean skills and malicious scripts
